_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS, hand-authored to exercise the LSAT Item contract. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests with redistribution licensing (D-SR11). Every item carries SyntheticFlag=SYNTHETIC.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] All registered voters who showed identification were allowed to vote. Since Maria was allowed to vote, she must have shown identification.
items.Stem: The reasoning above is flawed because it
items.ChoiceA: treats a sufficient condition as a necessary condition
items.ChoiceB: concludes that Maria is a registered voter without sufficient evidence
items.ChoiceC: assumes that only registered voters would want to vote
items.ChoiceD: relies on an unverified premise about voter identification requirements
items.ChoiceE: fails to consider that some voters may have been turned away
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Showing ID is sufficient to vote, but the argument treats it as necessary: because she voted, she must have shown ID. This reverses the conditional.
items.WhyWrongB: The argument doesn't conclude anything about registration status.
items.WhyWrongC: The argument says nothing about who wants to vote — this is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongD: The premise is stipulated as given, not unverified in context.
items.WhyWrongE: The argument is not about who was turned away; this is out of scope.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Studies show that cities with more libraries have lower crime rates than cities with fewer libraries. Therefore, building more libraries will reduce crime.
items.Stem: The argument is most vulnerable to the criticism that it
items.ChoiceA: overstates the magnitude of the relationship between libraries and crime
items.ChoiceB: ignores the possibility that crime rates have been declining nationally
items.ChoiceC: assumes that all libraries provide the same services
items.ChoiceD: mistakes a correlation for a causal relationship
items.ChoiceE: fails to consider that crime rates vary significantly by season
items.WhyWrongA: The argument does not make claims about magnitude — this misidentifies the flaw.
items.WhyWrongB: National trends could explain the correlation, but this choice describes a missing premise, not the flaw — close but less precise than C.
items.WhyWrongC: Library services are not relevant to this reasoning flaw.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. The argument observes a correlation (more libraries, lower crime) and infers a causal direction (libraries cause less crime) without ruling out alternative explanations.
items.WhyWrongE: Seasonal variation is unrelated to the argument's reasoning structure.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] I interviewed five students at a local university who all reported studying fewer than four hours per week. Clearly, university students today are not putting in adequate study time.
items.Stem: The argument above is most vulnerable to which of the following criticisms?
items.ChoiceA: It assumes that adequate study time is the same for all students.
items.ChoiceB: It draws a conclusion about all university students from a small, potentially unrepresentative sample.
items.ChoiceC: It presupposes that studying is the primary determinant of academic success.
items.ChoiceD: It does not consider that some students may work part-time jobs.
items.ChoiceE: It fails to specify what constitutes adequate study time.
items.WhyWrongA: The argument's flaw is about sample size, not about the definition of 'adequate' — though A is a real consideration, it is not the primary logical error.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Five students at one university cannot support a claim about all university students — hasty generalization from an unrepresentative sample.
items.WhyWrongC: The argument makes no claims about determinants of academic success.
items.WhyWrongD: Part-time work is out of scope for the argument's logical structure.
items.WhyWrongE: Lack of definition is a peripheral concern; the main flaw is the generalization.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The mayor's transit plan is the best option available for the city, because no other proposal on the table is as good as the mayor's plan.
items.Stem: The reasoning above is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it
items.ChoiceA: relies on the opinion of an authority whose judgment may be biased
items.ChoiceB: presumes, without providing justification, the very claim it sets out to establish
items.ChoiceC: assumes that a plan supported by the mayor must be unpopular with residents
items.ChoiceD: treats the failure of other proposals as positive proof of the plan's merits
items.ChoiceE: generalizes about all possible plans from a small number of past plans
items.WhyWrongA: No authority is cited; the mayor's judgment is not offered as evidence.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. 'Best because none is as good as it' just restates the conclusion as its own support — the argument goes in a circle.
items.WhyWrongC: Popularity is never mentioned; this is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongD: The argument does not reason from other proposals' failures; it merely asserts superiority.
items.WhyWrongE: No sample of past plans is used; the flaw is circularity, not overgeneralization.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Nothing is better than lifelong happiness. But a warm meal is better than nothing. Therefore, a warm meal is better than lifelong happiness.
items.Stem: The argument is flawed because it
items.ChoiceA: uses the word 'nothing' in two different senses
items.ChoiceB: draws a conclusion about happiness from claims solely about food
items.ChoiceC: treats a sufficient condition for satisfaction as a necessary one
items.ChoiceD: relies on a premise that most people would reject as false
items.ChoiceE: assumes that what is true of one warm meal is true of all warm meals
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. 'Nothing is better' (no thing surpasses it) and 'better than nothing' (better than having nothing) use 'nothing' in incompatible ways — an equivocation.
items.WhyWrongB: The subject matter is irrelevant; a valid argument could move between domains.
items.WhyWrongC: No conditional is involved, so the sufficient/necessary distinction does not apply.
items.WhyWrongD: Whether a premise seems false is beside the point; the structure is what fails.
items.WhyWrongE: No generalization across meals is made; the flaw is the shifting term.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Each individual player on the national squad is an outstanding athlete. Therefore, the national squad is an outstanding team.
items.Stem: The reasoning above is flawed because it
items.ChoiceA: assumes that an outstanding team must be composed of outstanding players
items.ChoiceB: assumes that what is true of each member of a group must be true of the group as a whole
items.ChoiceC: fails to specify the criteria by which an athlete is judged outstanding
items.ChoiceD: concludes that the team will therefore win every one of its upcoming matches
items.ChoiceE: relies on the individual players' own assessments of how skilled they are
items.WhyWrongA: This is the converse of the argument's move and is not what it assumes.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Team quality depends on coordination, not just individual talent; inferring the whole from its parts is the composition fallacy.
items.WhyWrongC: Defining 'outstanding' more precisely would not repair the part-to-whole leap.
items.WhyWrongD: The conclusion is about being outstanding, not about winning specific matches.
items.WhyWrongE: No testimony from the players is used.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] To balance the budget we must either cut the education fund or raise income taxes. Since raising income taxes would slow economic growth, we must cut the education fund.
items.Stem: The argument's reasoning is most vulnerable to criticism because it
items.ChoiceA: assumes without support that raising income taxes always slows growth
items.ChoiceB: relies on a questionable analogy between a budget and a household's finances
items.ChoiceC: attacks the motives of those who favor higher income taxes
items.ChoiceD: presents two courses of action as the only options when others may be available
items.ChoiceE: concludes that cutting the education fund will itself accelerate growth
items.WhyWrongA: This targets a premise's truth, not the two-options structure that is the flaw.
items.WhyWrongC: No one's motives are attacked in the argument.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. The budget could be balanced by other means (spending cuts elsewhere, other revenue); framing only two options is a false dichotomy.
items.WhyWrongE: The argument never claims cutting education boosts growth.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Dr. Alvarez argues, citing her lab results, that the new drug is safe. But Dr. Alvarez was paid by the drug's manufacturer, so her claim that the drug is safe should be dismissed.
items.Stem: The argument is most vulnerable to the criticism that it
items.ChoiceA: fails to consider that other researchers might reach the same conclusion
items.ChoiceB: confuses the safety of the drug with its effectiveness at treating illness
items.ChoiceC: rejects a claim by criticizing its source rather than addressing the evidence for it
items.ChoiceD: treats being paid by the manufacturer as conclusive proof that the claim is false
items.ChoiceE: assumes that a drug endorsed by a physician must therefore be dangerous
items.WhyWrongA: Whether others agree is not what the argument overlooks; it attacks the source.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. The payment bears on Alvarez's motives, not on whether her lab results support safety; dismissing the claim on that basis is an ad hominem.
items.WhyWrongD: This is close, but the flaw is attacking the source, not specifically treating payment as proof of falsity.
items.WhyWrongE: The argument does not conclude the drug is dangerous, only that the claim should be dismissed.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Senator Ito has proposed a modest reduction in the military budget. But leaving our nation defenseless against every conceivable threat would be reckless. We should therefore reject the senator's proposal.
items.Stem: The critic's reasoning is most vulnerable to criticism because it
items.ChoiceA: relies on the testimony of unnamed military experts
items.ChoiceB: appeals to fear in place of offering any relevant evidence
items.ChoiceC: recasts the senator's position as far more extreme than it is and then attacks that distortion
items.ChoiceD: presumes, without evidence, that the senator's proposal has no supporters
items.ChoiceE: concludes that any reduction in military spending is dangerous
items.WhyWrongB: There is a whiff of fear, but the identifiable flaw is the misrepresentation of the position.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. 'Modest reduction' is turned into 'defenseless against every threat' — a caricature — and the caricature, not the actual proposal, is refuted.
items.WhyWrongD: Supporter counts are irrelevant and not assumed.
items.WhyWrongE: The argument targets only this proposal, and only via the distortion; it does not claim all reductions are dangerous.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A celebrated film actor has declared that this brand of vitamin supplement prevents the common cold. Because the actor is admired by millions, the supplement must indeed prevent colds.
items.Stem: The reasoning above is flawed because it
items.ChoiceA: relies on the endorsement of someone who has no relevant medical expertise
items.ChoiceB: assumes that a product admired by millions of people must be effective
items.ChoiceC: fails to state how many colds the supplement has actually prevented
items.ChoiceD: treats preventing colds as sufficient for overall good health
items.ChoiceE: takes for granted that the actor personally uses the supplement
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Fame in acting confers no authority about medicine, so the endorsement provides no support for the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongB: The argument rests on the actor's endorsement, not on the product's popularity.
items.WhyWrongC: A missing count does not identify the reasoning error; the flaw is the misplaced authority.
items.WhyWrongD: Overall health is out of scope; the claim is only about colds.
items.WhyWrongE: Personal use is neither claimed nor assumed.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Running a country is essentially like running a business. Successful businesses cut costs wherever possible to maximize profit, so a successful government should cut public services wherever possible to maximize its budget surplus.
items.Stem: The argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it
items.ChoiceA: takes for granted that every successful business cuts costs wherever possible
items.ChoiceB: concludes that public services provide no real value to the citizens who use them
items.ChoiceC: relies uncritically on the opinions of a few prominent business leaders
items.ChoiceD: assumes that because governments and businesses are alike in some respects, they are alike in the respect at issue
items.ChoiceE: confuses cutting an organization's costs with increasing its overall revenue
items.WhyWrongA: Even granting this premise, the analogy from business to government would still fail.
items.WhyWrongB: The argument never claims services are worthless; it treats surplus as the goal.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. A government's purpose (serving citizens) differs from a business's (profit) in exactly the respect the argument turns on, so the analogy breaks down.
items.WhyWrongE: The argument does not equate cost-cutting with revenue; the flaw is the analogy.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A recent survey found that most residents want the city to improve public transportation. Clearly, then, most residents would support a large property-tax increase to build a new subway line.
items.Stem: The argument is flawed because it
items.ChoiceA: shifts from residents' general wish for improvement to support for one specific and costly means of achieving it
items.ChoiceB: concludes that a subway is the best possible form of public transportation
items.ChoiceC: treats wanting better transit as sufficient grounds for opposing all taxes
items.ChoiceD: assumes that the survey's sample of residents was unrepresentative
items.ChoiceE: generalizes about all residents from the responses of only a few
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Wanting improvement in general does not entail support for a particular expensive subway funded by a tax hike; the conclusion's scope exceeds the premise.
items.WhyWrongC: This contradicts the stimulus, which infers support, not opposition, regarding taxes.
items.WhyWrongD: The argument treats the survey as representative; it does not assume the opposite.
items.WhyWrongE: The premise already speaks of 'most residents'; overgeneralizing from a few is not the move.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] To earn an A in the course, a student must complete the final project. Jordan completed the final project, so Jordan will earn an A.
items.Stem: The reasoning above is flawed because it
items.ChoiceA: draws a conclusion about all students from the case of a single student
items.ChoiceB: assumes that only students who earn an A complete the final project
items.ChoiceC: treats a condition that is merely necessary for earning an A as if it were sufficient
items.ChoiceD: presumes that the final project was especially difficult to complete
items.ChoiceE: overlooks the possibility that Jordan performed poorly on other coursework
items.WhyWrongA: The conclusion is only about Jordan, not all students.
items.WhyWrongB: That is the converse of the stated rule and is not what the argument assumes.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Completing the project is required for an A but does not guarantee one; the argument wrongly treats the necessary condition as sufficient.
items.WhyWrongE: This is a real-world consideration, but the identifiable logical error is the necessary/sufficient confusion.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Neighborhoods that have more police officers on patrol tend to report more crimes than neighborhoods with fewer officers. Therefore, increasing the number of officers on patrol causes the crime rate to rise.
items.Stem: The argument is most vulnerable to the criticism that it
items.ChoiceA: concludes that police officers themselves are committing the crimes
items.ChoiceB: infers a causal relationship from a correlation, ignoring that high crime may prompt more policing
items.ChoiceC: relies on data drawn from an unrepresentative set of neighborhoods
items.ChoiceD: assumes that reported crime figures accurately reflect the true crime rate
items.ChoiceE: treats the presence of police as a necessary condition for crime to occur
items.WhyWrongA: The argument never claims officers commit the crimes; this overreads the conclusion.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The direction of causation is likely reversed (crime attracts police), and other factors may drive both; a correlation alone does not establish the stated cause.
items.WhyWrongC: The flaw is the causal inference, not the representativeness of the neighborhoods sampled.
items.WhyWrongD: Reporting accuracy is a side issue; the core error is the causal leap.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The last three chief executives the firm hired from outside the company each resigned within a year. Therefore, hiring any executive from outside the company is a mistake.
items.Stem: The reasoning above is most vulnerable to criticism because it
items.ChoiceA: draws a sweeping conclusion from a very small number of cases
items.ChoiceB: presumes that resigning within a year proves poor job performance
items.ChoiceC: concludes that executives promoted from within always succeed
items.ChoiceD: assumes that the three executives all resigned for the same reason
items.ChoiceE: treats a claim about executives as a claim about all employees
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Three cases cannot support a universal judgment about all outside hires — a hasty generalization from an inadequate sample.
items.WhyWrongB: It never equates early resignation with poor performance.
items.WhyWrongC: The argument makes no claim about internal hires.
items.WhyWrongD: Shared reasons are not assumed; the error is the small-sample leap.
items.WhyWrongE: It stays within 'executives'; it does not shift to all employees.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The hiring committee, taken as a whole, made a wise decision. It follows that each member of the committee is wise.
items.Stem: The argument is flawed because it
items.ChoiceA: takes for granted that a wise decision requires the members to agree unanimously
items.ChoiceB: assumes that a property of a group as a whole must be a property of each of its members
items.ChoiceC: concludes that the committee's decision was popular with those affected by it
items.ChoiceD: generalizes about all hiring committees from this single committee
items.ChoiceE: overlooks the possibility that the committee's decision was actually a mistake
items.WhyWrongA: Unanimity is neither required nor assumed.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. A group can decide wisely through debate even if no single member is individually wise; inferring the members from the whole is the division fallacy.
items.WhyWrongD: The conclusion is about this committee's members, not all committees.
items.WhyWrongE: The premise stipulates the decision was wise, so this contradicts it.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The light now reaching Earth from a distant galaxy left it billions of years ago, so a telescope shows that galaxy only as it was in the remote past. In those images it teems with young, hot, short-lived stars. The astronomer concludes that the galaxy is teeming with such stars today.
items.Stem: The astronomer's reasoning is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it
items.ChoiceA: presumes, without warrant, that most galaxies are filled with young, hot stars
items.ChoiceB: overlooks the possibility that the telescopes used were improperly calibrated
items.ChoiceC: draws a conclusion about the galaxy's present from evidence concerning only its remote past
items.ChoiceD: takes for granted that young, hot stars are necessarily short-lived ones
items.ChoiceE: assumes that light from every galaxy takes the same amount of time to arrive
items.WhyWrongA: The argument makes no claim about most galaxies, only about this one.
items.WhyWrongB: Calibration of the telescopes is never at issue in the reasoning.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Short-lived stars seen as they were billions of years ago need not still exist now; the premises describe only the galaxy's remote past.
items.WhyWrongD: Whether such stars are short-lived is consistent with the argument and is not its error.
items.WhyWrongE: The argument does not infer any star's age from its brightness.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Critic: A language is living only if it changes over time. Latin, everyone agrees, is a dead language. Yet the Latin written by scholars today uses vocabulary that classical Latin lacked. So, contrary to the consensus, Latin is in fact a living language.
items.Stem: The critic's reasoning is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it
items.ChoiceA: questions the honesty of the scholars whose usage it cites
items.ChoiceB: assumes that any language undergoing change must have many speakers
items.ChoiceC: treats the widespread belief that Latin is dead as proof that it is dead
items.ChoiceD: treats a kind of change that has no bearing on whether a language is living as if it did
items.ChoiceE: concludes that Latin lives merely because it was once in wide use
items.WhyWrongA: The argument relies on the scholars' reported usage; it does not impugn them.
items.WhyWrongB: No claim about the number of speakers is made or required.
items.WhyWrongC: The argument rejects that belief rather than resting on it.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. The criterion's 'change' is the natural evolution of a spoken tongue; scholars adding vocabulary is a different sort of change and does not satisfy it.
items.WhyWrongE: Past prevalence plays no role in the inference.
